article.title: Japan Airlines Begins Two-Year Humanoid Robot Trial at Haneda, Pairing Unitree G1 Units With Ground Crews to Confront a Labor Shortage
article.summary: JAL Ground Service and GMO AI & Robotics will deploy humanoid robots on the apron at Tokyo's Haneda starting in May, in what local press call Japan's first such airport trial.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Japan Airlines and GMO Internet Group announced on April 27 that they will begin a multi-year demonstration trial of humanoid robots on the apron at Tokyo's Haneda Airport beginning in May, according to [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/29/japan_airport_robot_trial/). The pilot, run jointly by JAL Ground Service and GMO AI & Robotics, will start with two China-built humanoid units performing cargo-handling tasks alongside human ground crews and is scheduled to continue through 2028, as reported by [Japan Today](https://japantoday.com/category/tech/jal-gmo-to-test-using-humanoid-robots-for-airport-ground-operations) citing Kyodo News. Local press, including [Aviation A2Z](https://aviationa2z.com/index.php/2026/04/29/this-airline-is-using-humanoid-robots-at-tokyo-haneda-airport/), describe the program as Japan's first such initiative in airport ground handling.

## What We Know

The trial pairs JAL Ground Service Co. — the JAL subsidiary responsible for ramp and baggage operations — with GMO AI & Robotics, the trading arm GMO Internet Group set up to commercialize humanoid systems. The companies announced the project at a press event on Monday, April 27, [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/29/japan_airport_robot_trial/) reported.

According to [Aviation A2Z](https://aviationa2z.com/index.php/2026/04/29/this-airline-is-using-humanoid-robots-at-tokyo-haneda-airport/), two humanoid robots will be deployed initially. Their first assigned tasks are narrow: transporting cargo containers and operating the mechanical levers that lock those containers in place. [Japan Today](https://japantoday.com/category/tech/jal-gmo-to-test-using-humanoid-robots-for-airport-ground-operations) reported via Kyodo that the project is scheduled to run through 2028, with [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/29/japan_airport_robot_trial/) framing the duration as a "two-year testing period" beginning in May. Both [AeroXplorer](https://aeroxplorer.com/articles/japan-airlines-is-sending-humanoid-robots-onto-the-tarmac-at-haneda.php) and [Aviation A2Z](https://aviationa2z.com/index.php/2026/04/29/this-airline-is-using-humanoid-robots-at-tokyo-haneda-airport/) characterize the trial as an approximately three-year exercise with phased verification. The 2028 end date and the May 2026 start are the points of agreement across all sources.

The robots themselves are Chinese-built Unitree G1 units, according to [Interesting Engineering](https://interestingengineering.com/ai-robotics/japan-humanoid-robots-haneda-airport) and [New Atlas](https://newatlas.com/ai-humanoids/humanoid-robots-baggage-handlers-tokyo-airport-unitree/). [Interesting Engineering](https://interestingengineering.com/ai-robotics/japan-humanoid-robots-haneda-airport) describes the G1 as a 1.32-meter, 35-kilogram bipedal platform with 23 degrees of freedom, 3D LiDAR and depth-camera sensing, and a 9,000 mAh battery rated for up to two hours of continuous operation. [Japan Today](https://japantoday.com/category/tech/jal-gmo-to-test-using-humanoid-robots-for-airport-ground-operations), citing the partners' own briefing, said the units "can currently operate continuously for two to three hours."

The rationale offered by JAL is built around a labor-shortage argument that has been repeated across the Japanese aviation sector for several years. [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/29/japan_airport_robot_trial/) quoted JAL's reasoning that "the myriad pieces of equipment used airside at airports were all designed to be used by humans. It therefore makes sense to try humanoid robots that can use existing tools, rather than try to develop specific bots for each job." In the same announcement, JAL framed the goal of the program as "to realize a sustainable operational structure through labor savings and workload reduction," per [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/29/japan_airport_robot_trial/) and an identical statement reported by [Interesting Engineering](https://interestingengineering.com/ai-robotics/japan-humanoid-robots-haneda-airport).

GMO AI & Robotics president Tomohiro Uchida told reporters that "while airports appear highly automated and standardized, their back-end operations still rely heavily on human labor," according to [Japan Today](https://japantoday.com/category/tech/jal-gmo-to-test-using-humanoid-robots-for-airport-ground-operations). [Futurism](https://futurism.com/robots-and-machines/japan-trialing-humanoid-robots-baggage-handlers) carried a similar version of the quote attributed to Uchida via BBC reporting. JAL Ground Service president Yoshiteru Suzuki said the deployment "is likely to inevitably reduce workers' burden, providing significant benefits to employees," per [Japan Today](https://japantoday.com/category/tech/jal-gmo-to-test-using-humanoid-robots-for-airport-ground-operations).

The ground-handling staff base the partners are aiming to support is sizable. [AeroXplorer](https://aeroxplorer.com/articles/japan-airlines-is-sending-humanoid-robots-onto-the-tarmac-at-haneda.php) and [Aviation A2Z](https://aviationa2z.com/index.php/2026/04/29/this-airline-is-using-humanoid-robots-at-tokyo-haneda-airport/) both report that JAL employs roughly 4,000 ground handling staff. Haneda itself, where the trial will take place, processes more than 60 million passengers a year, according to [New Atlas](https://newatlas.com/ai-humanoids/humanoid-robots-baggage-handlers-tokyo-airport-unitree/) and [Futurism](https://futurism.com/robots-and-machines/japan-trialing-humanoid-robots-baggage-handlers).

## What the Robots Will Not Do, Yet

While the partners' messaging gestures at a long list of potential roles, the early scope is deliberately restricted. [Aviation A2Z](https://aviationa2z.com/index.php/2026/04/29/this-airline-is-using-humanoid-robots-at-tokyo-haneda-airport/) reports that the broader vision includes baggage loading, cabin cleaning, and ground support equipment handling, but those are framed as future expansions rather than initial duties. [Japan Today](https://japantoday.com/category/tech/jal-gmo-to-test-using-humanoid-robots-for-airport-ground-operations) likewise reports the partners plan to expand robot use "to other areas, including aircraft cabin cleaning," only after the initial cargo phase.

The humanoid units shown in the demonstration footage have visible limits. [Futurism](https://futurism.com/robots-and-machines/japan-trialing-humanoid-robots-baggage-handlers) noted that in the rollout demo the robot "barely touches" a container that is mostly moved by a conveyor belt — a candid framing of where the technology actually sits today. The two-hour battery rating cited by [Interesting Engineering](https://interestingengineering.com/ai-robotics/japan-humanoid-robots-haneda-airport) is also a real operational constraint at an airport where ground crews work continuous shifts under tight aircraft turnarounds.

The partners have repeatedly stressed that the humans are not being replaced. [AeroXplorer](https://aeroxplorer.com/articles/japan-airlines-is-sending-humanoid-robots-onto-the-tarmac-at-haneda.php) reports that JAL and GMO have been "deliberate in framing the robots as complementary to human workers rather than as replacements."

## What We Don't Know

Neither company has disclosed the unit cost of the robots procured for the trial, the precise number that will be deployed beyond the initial pair, or the criteria that will determine whether the program is extended, scaled, or terminated. Reporting across outlets has occasionally diverged on supplier identification: [Interesting Engineering](https://interestingengineering.com/ai-robotics/japan-humanoid-robots-haneda-airport) and [New Atlas](https://newatlas.com/ai-humanoids/humanoid-robots-baggage-handlers-tokyo-airport-unitree/) identify the platform as a Unitree G1, while [The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/04/29/japan_airport_robot_trial/) notes that GMO offers a lineup of humanoid models — only one of which it described as suitable for outdoor use.

The geopolitical question of an airline operator running ground-side trials with Chinese-built robots in a country that is increasingly attentive to supply-chain provenance has not been addressed in the partners' public materials, and none of the cited sources report a comment from Japan's transport ministry on the trial.

Finally, the labor-relations implications are unstated. The partners' formulation that the project will "reduce workers' burden" while delivering "benefits to employees," per [Japan Today](https://japantoday.com/category/tech/jal-gmo-to-test-using-humanoid-robots-for-airport-ground-operations), leaves open how a successful trial would change headcount at JAL Ground Service, which today employs the roughly 4,000-person workforce reported by [AeroXplorer](https://aeroxplorer.com/articles/japan-airlines-is-sending-humanoid-robots-onto-the-tarmac-at-haneda.php).

## Context

Japan's commercial humanoid story has been dominated to date by industrial assembly and consumer demonstrations, and the global market has been overwhelmingly Chinese in volume. Unitree, whose G1 platform JAL is now putting on the apron, is the same manufacturer The Machine Herald has tracked in the context of the broader Chinese lead in humanoid shipments. The Haneda trial pushes the platform into a new operational setting — outdoors, on a live tarmac, around aircraft and cargo systems — where reliability and safety constraints are very different from a factory floor or a research lab. Whether a 35-kilogram, two-hour-battery bipedal robot can do useful work in that environment, even at narrowly scoped tasks, is the empirical question the next two years are designed to answer.
